meta.title: Studying in Europe — EU Student Visa Guide
meta.description: How to study in Europe legally — EU student visa, free universities in Germany, scholarships, and Erasmus+.

hero.title: Studying in Europe
hero.sub: Europe is home to some of the world's best universities — and many charge little or no tuition. Germany, Norway, and Finland have no tuition fees for international students. With an EU Student Visa, you can study, work part-time, and build a future legally.

alert.text: You can study at a top European university for free. German public universities charge only a €150–300/semester administration fee — not tuition. DAAD scholarships cover living costs. This is a real, accessible route that costs a fraction of irregular migration.

options.desc: Public universities in Germany charge no tuition fees for international students — only a semester fee (~€150–300). With 300+ English-taught programmes and strong job prospects, Germany is the top choice for international students in Europe.

French public universities charge very low tuition fees. Paris, Lyon, and Toulouse are world-class university cities. Many programmes taught in English. French-speaking applicants have additional options.
Norwegian public universities charge no tuition fees for any nationality. The cost of living is high, but scholarships and part-time work can cover it.
Tuition fees exist but Finland offers extensive scholarships for international students. Strong engineering and technology programmes.
Higher tuition but excellent English-taught programmes. Strong scholarship programmes from universities and the Dutch government (Holland Scholarship).

stepsTitle: How to apply for an EU Student Visa

steps.title: Apply to a university and get accepted
steps.desc: Research universities using the DAAD database (Germany), Campus France (France), or the Nuffic guide for Netherlands. Apply through the university directly. Scholarships are competitive — apply early.

steps.title: Secure funding
steps.desc: Apply for DAAD scholarships (Germany), Erasmus+ Mundus, Eiffel Scholarships (France), or university-specific grants. A scholarship removes almost all visa obstacles.

steps.title: Apply for a student visa at the embassy
steps.desc: Apply at the relevant EU country's embassy or consulate. Requirements include acceptance letter, proof of funds (€700–900/month), health insurance, and accommodation proof.

steps.title: Arrive and register
steps.desc: Register at the local municipality within the first week. Enrol at your university. Open a local bank account. Register with the health insurance provider.

steps.title: Work part-time and gain experience
steps.desc: EU student visas allow up to 20 hours/week part-time work during studies. Use this to build European work experience.

steps.title: Stay after graduation
steps.desc: Germany allows 18 months after graduation to find a job. After finding a job, switch to a Blue Card or other work permit. Many graduates stay and build careers in Europe legally.

scholarships.name: DAAD Scholarships
scholarships.desc: German Academic Exchange Service — extensive scholarship programmes for international students in Germany

scholarships.name: Erasmus+ Mundus
scholarships.desc: EU-funded scholarships for international students to study at European university consortia

scholarships.name: Eiffel Excellence Scholarship (France)
scholarships.desc: French government scholarship for Master's and PhD students

scholarships.name: Holland Scholarship
scholarships.desc: Dutch government scholarship for students from outside the EU
